item.title: Best School Accounting Software in 2026: Features, Reviews & Pricing
item.content:encoded:
Accounting for educational institutions, such as universities and K-12 schools, can be complex. Managing income or expenses can quickly become unwieldy, and manual processes can make this process time-consuming and error-prone.  

Traditional small business accounting tools fail educational institutions by not accounting for the intricacies your school demands. School fund accounting software helps your institute manage funds across multiple departments and campuses, highlights grant compliance and keeps your school audit compliant. But what is the best school accounting software?  

This guide explores the best school accounting software in 2026, considering unique features, pricing, benefits, and reviews.  

What Is School Accounting Software? 

School fund accounting software provides a purpose-built solution to meet the unique financial requirements of educational institutions. These tools allow teams to track unlimited funding sources, such as grants and donations, with absolute precision, ensuring strict regulatory compliance.   

School accounting software eliminates manual workarounds and significantly reduces the risk of human error, providing real-time visibility into operational effectiveness, empowering your organization to support educational goals at every level.  

When evaluating options, consider:  

  • Fund and grant tracking across programs, departments, and campuses  
  • Budget management tools  
  • Compliance and audit trail for education finance regulations 
  • Payroll, HR, and student information systems integrations  
  • Reporting flexibility for administrators, boards, and auditors 

What Is the Best School Accounting Software? 

  • Best School Accounting Software Overall — MIP Accounting by Momentive Software 
  • Best for K-12 Schools — PowerSchool ERP 
  • Best for Charter Schools — Sage Intacct 
  • Best for Private and Independent Schools — Blackbaud Financial Edge NXT 
  • Best for Small Schools and Single-Site Campuses — Aplos 
  • Best for Multi-Campus Schools — Skyward Business Suite 
  • Best Free School Accounting Software — Zoho Books 

Best Overall School Accounting Software 

MIP Accounting by Momentive Software 

MIP Accounting by Momentive Software is a comprehensive fund accounting platform specifically engineered to handle the intricate financial needs of educational institutions. From K-12 districts to private academies, it simplifies complex operations while supporting strict regulatory compliance, multi-campus accounting, and granular fund tracking. 

Key Features 

  • Advanced accounting built for a complex school fund and grant structure  
  • Robust reporting and analytics for total financial transparency across departments 
  • Seamless integration with student information systems, enrollment platforms, and advancement tools 
  • Cloud-based architecture for remote administrative flexibility and data security 
  • Comprehensive payroll and HR management tools tailored for educational staffing 
  • Dedicated implementation services and customer support focused on the education sector 

Reviews 

School business officers and finance directors praise MIP Accounting for its depth, reliability, and adaptability. Many highlight the platform’s unique ability to manage restricted grants and multi-campus budgets with absolute precision, saving staff hours of manual reconciliation and spreadsheet management. 

Benefits 

  • Centralizes complex financial operations for multiple campuses in one platform 
  • Delivers accurate, audit-ready financial statements and grant management 
  • Improves administrative efficiency through sophisticated workflow automation 
  • Scales seamlessly with your institution as enrollment and funding sources grow  

Pricing 

MIP Accounting offers tiered pricing based on the size of your school and specific feature requirements, with transparent options for cloud-based or on-premise deployment to fit your IT infrastructure. 

Why We Picked It 

MIP Accounting by Momentive Software is the top choice for educational institutions and school districts seeking a powerful, purpose-built accounting system. It perfectly balances depth, flexibility, and compliance with a user-friendly interface, illustrating why school leaders trust MIP Accounting to manage their most critical financial data. 

[elementor-template id="1458"]

Best for K-12 Schools 

PowerSchool ERP 

Overview 

PowerSchool ERP is the cornerstone of K-12 district management, offering a unified ecosystem that bridges the gap between the classroom and the business office. It is specifically built to handle the data requirements and regulatory standards of large public school districts. 

Key Features 

  • Unified Student Information System and Enterprise Resource Planning integration 
  • Automated state and federal compliance reporting 
  • Advanced human resources and payroll management for large-scale staffing 
  • Real-time budgeting and expense tracking at the district and school level 
  • Secure mobile access for employees and administrators 

Reviews 

Administrators consistently rate PowerSchool highly for its ability to centralize fragmented data. Users appreciate the depth of the reporting tools, noting that while the system is complex, it provides  district-wide visibility that other platforms cannot match. 

Benefits 

  • Eliminates manual data imports between student and financial records  
  • Reduces administrative burnout through automated workflows 
  • Ensures funding accuracy with precise enrollment-linked reporting 
  • Provides a 360-degree view of district financial health 

Pricing 

PowerSchool typically operates on a custom quote basis determined by district size and selected modules. Specialized teacher-centric modules can start as low as $4.95 per month, while full ERP suites require a subscription agreement. 

Why We Picked It 

PowerSchool ERP is one of the few platforms capable of scaling to the needs of thousands of students and staff members while maintaining native integration between educational and financial data. 

Best for Charter Schools

Sage Intacct 

Overview 

Sage Intacct provides a cloud-based financial management solution tailored for charter school networks. It excels at multi-entity accounting, enabling charter leaders to manage several campuses under a single umbrella with ease. 

Key Features 

  • AI-powered Copilot integration for automated data analysis and narratives 
  • Multidimensional chart of accounts for tracking performance across various entities 
  • Automated revenue recognition for tuition and grants 
  • Customizable dashboards for real-time stakeholder visibility 
  • Robust audit trails and internal controls for strict compliance 

Reviews 

Finance professionals in the charter sector praise Sage Intacct for its modern interface and powerful automation. Reviewers frequently mention the time saved during monthly closes and the ease of generating board-ready reports. 

Benefits 

  • Streamlines multi-campus management without complex file structures 
  • Reduces human error through AI-driven reconciliation 
  • Improves financial agility with real-time data insights 
  • Simplifies the audit process with granular transaction tracking 

Pricing 

Sage Intacct generally ranges from $15,000 to $30,000 annually, depending on the complexity of the organization and the number of users. 

Why We Picked It 

We chose Sage Intacct for its superior multi-entity management capabilities and its commitment to integrating AI, which allows growing networks to scale without exponentially increasing their back-office staff. 

Best for Private and Independent Schools

Blackbaud Financial Edge NXT  

Blackbaud Financial Edge NXT is the gold standard for independent schools where donor relationships and tuition management are critical. It is a fund accounting system designed to build trust with funders and ensure every dollar is used according to its restrictions. 

Key Features 

  • Seamless integration with Raiser’s Edge NXT for fundraising and finance alignment  
  • AI-automated reconciliation to speed up the time to close 
  • Flexible sub-fund functionality for tracking grants, projects, and endowments 
  • Mobile-friendly payment portals for families and donors 
  • Role-based access to ensure data security and separation of duties  

Reviews 

Private school business officers value the platform for its unmatched accountability. Users highlight the convenience of the cloud-based system and the excellent training resources provided during the implementation phase. 

Benefits 

  • Maximizes fund utilization with granular tracking 
  • Strengthens donor trust through transparent impact reporting 
  • Reduces tuition delinquency with automated payment reminders and mobile options 
  • Improves efficiency by connecting development and finance teams 

Pricing 

Blackbaud uses a personalized pricing model tailored to the specific needs of the school. Interested institutions should request a breakdown to receive a quote based on their enrollment and feature requirements. 

Why We Picked It 

Blackbaud specializes in the intersection of tuition management, donor stewardship, and fund accounting, providing a connected ecosystem that few other vendors can offer. 

Best for Small Schools and Single-Site Campuses 

Aplos 

Overview 

Aplos is a cloud-based platform designed specifically for small schools and single-site campuses that need robust fund accounting without the complexity of enterprise-level software. It prioritizes ease of use for small teams and volunteers. 

Key Features 

  • Specialized fund accounting ledgers for restricted and unrestricted funds  
  • Integrated donation tracking and online giving forms 
  • Budgeting and financial reporting templates for boards and auditors 
  • Unlimited user seats for volunteers (on select roles) 
  • Website builder and member database integrations 

Reviews 

Aplos is a user-friendly option for those without an extensive accounting background. Reviewers appreciate the professional customer support and the clear, jargon-free interface. 

Benefits 

  • Lower barrier to entry with a quick onboarding process 
  • Centralizes giving and accounting in one simple platform 
  • Ensures transparency with real-time balance sheets by fund 
  • Cost-effective for schools with limited administrative budgets 

Pricing 

Aplos pricing is transparent, with the Lite plan starting at $79 per month and the Core plan at $99 per month. A 15-day free trial is available for new users. 

Why We Picked It 

Aplos removes the technical friction of fund accounting. It allows smaller institutions to maintain professional-grade financial standards without requiring a full-time IT or accounting department. 

Best for Multi-Campus Schools

Skyward Business Suite 

Overview 

Skyward Business Suite offers a powerful dual solution that integrates student management with essential back-office functions like payroll, human resources, and financial management. It is designed for public schools and districts that require centralized oversight of multiple campuses. 

Key Features 

  • Consolidated general ledger with real-time data updates across all sites 
  • Comprehensive student services integration (behavior, health, and food services) 
  • Advanced payroll and human resources modules 
  • Centralized enrollment and fee management 
  • Role-based reporting for campus leaders and district administrators 

Reviews 

Large-scale users appreciate the stability and comprehensiveness of the Skyward ecosystem. While some users find the initial setup complex, they often report that the long-term benefits of having all district data in one place are undeniable. 

Benefits 

  • Automates manual workflows across several locations 
  • Promotes family engagement through a unified parent and student portal 
  • Protects the district with robust data privacy and security systems 
  • Improves decision-making with district-wide financial transparency 

Pricing 

Skyward provides custom quotes based on the size of the district and the specific suites chosen. Estimates for larger districts often start around $30,000 per year. 

Why We Picked It 

Skyward was selected for multi-campus schools due to its ability to maintain a single source of truth for both student and financial data across regional consortia, ensuring that no campus becomes an information silo.  

Best Free School Accounting Software 

Zoho Books 

Overview 

Zoho Books is a high-performance accounting tool that offers a robust free tier for micro-schools and startup educational initiatives. It provides a bridge to digital financial maturity for those operating on a shoestring budget. 

Key Features 

  • Comprehensive invoicing and expense tracking 
  • Automated payment reminders and late fee management 
  • Client portal for parents to view and pay invoices 
  • Bank reconciliation and automated journal entries 
  • Integration with the broader Zoho ecosystem (CRM, Mail, and Desk) 

Reviews 

Reviewers praise Zoho Books for being intuitive and fast. Micro-school founders appreciate the professional templates and the ease with which they can transition from paper records to a digital ledger. 

Benefits 

  • Zero cost for schools with revenue under $50,000 annually 
  •  Simplifies the accounts receivable cycle with online payment options 
  • Scales seamlessly with paid tiers as the school grows  
  • Reduces paperwork with mobile app accessibility 

Pricing 

The basic plan is Free for businesses with revenue under $50,000. Paid tiers for larger schools range from $20 to $275 per month, offering more users and advanced features. 

Why We Picked It 

Zoho Books is the best free option because it does not compromise on core accounting functionality. It gives new or micro-schools a professional platform to establish sound financial habits without the burden of an upfront software investment. 

Choosing the Right School Accounting Software 

Whether you are managing a single-site private school, a growing charter network, or a massive K-12 district, your software must move beyond basic income and expense tracking to handle the complexities of restricted grants, multi-campus reporting, and strict regulatory compliance.  

School fund accounting software eliminates the manual data shuffle and administrative burnout by integrating seamlessly with your existing student information systems and payroll tools. 

Ultimately, the best choice is one that provides a 360-degree view of your institutional health, ensuring every dollar is tracked with the precision necessary to satisfy auditors and protect your mission of student success.

[elementor-template id="1458"]

FAQ 

What Is the Best Accounting Software for Schools?

While the best choice depends on your specific needs, MIP Accounting is widely considered the best overall for its depth and fund-tracking capabilities. For large public districts, PowerSchool ERP is a top contender, while Aplos is often the favorite for small, single-site campuses. 

What Is the Difference Between a Regular and School Accounting Software?

Regular accounting software is built for for-profit businesses to track net income and loss. School accounting software is built for fund accounting, which focuses on accountability and stewardship. It allows schools to track money based on its source and intended use (such as a specific grant or scholarship fund) rather than just broad categories of income and expense.  

Do Schools Need Fund Accounting Software?

Yes. Because schools receive restricted funds (money that must be spent on specific programs or initiatives), they are legally and ethically required to prove those funds were used correctly. Regular software often requires manual workarounds to track these restrictions, which increases the risk of human error and audit failure. 

What Accounting Software Do School Districts Use?

Large-scale school districts typically use Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) systems like PowerSchool, Skyward, or Infinite Campus. These platforms are designed to handle the massive complexity of thousands of employees and state-level compliance reporting. 

How Much Does School Accounting Software Cost? 

The cost varies significantly based on scale. Micro-schools can start with free options like Zoho Books (for revenue under $50,000), while mid-sized schools might pay between $1,500 and $10,000 annually. Large enterprise or district-wide solutions often start at $30,000 per year or more, depending on the number of modules and students.

item.content:encoded:
ASAE’s Association Management System (AMS) Demo Days is a premier virtual event for associations seeking cutting-edge technology solutions. This can’t-miss event provides a unique opportunity to explore, compare, and evaluate leading AMS platforms—all in one place, without the hassle of scheduling multiple vendor meetings. 

ASAE’s AMS Demo Days is a two-day, virtual event held on April 22 and April 23, 2026, from 9 a.m. to 4 p.m. ET. As a strategic Partner of ASAE, Momentive Software is excited to showcase our industry-leading AMS platforms: Nimble AMS, Cobalt AMS, NetForum, Aptify, and YourMembership—each designed to bring on better experiences for your staff and members. 

[elementor-template id="1458"]

Why attend ASAE’s AMS Demo Days? 

Wondering why you should attend ASAE’s AMS Demo Days? Whether you’re actively searching for a new AMS or simply exploring options for the future, ASAE’s AMS Demo Days is your one-stop shop for making informed technology decisions. Here are the top three reasons you shouldn’t miss this event: 

  • Effortless AMS comparisons

  • Expert insights

  • Ask the right questions

[elementor-template id="1526"]

Find the right AMS for your association 

Choosing the right AMS is a critical decision that impacts your organization’s efficiency, member engagement, and overall success. You’ll want to search for an AMS that meets your organization’s unique needs.

Live walkthroughs 

Each demo provides a real-time tour of the AMS platform, showcasing its layout, navigation, and core functionalities. You’ll see how each system works—from managing memberships to processing payments and running reports. This hands-on approach helps you visualize how the AMS could fit into your association’s daily operations. 

Key features and benefits

    How the software connects with your existing tools, such as event platforms or learning management software, to enhance efficiency 

Understanding these features will help you determine which AMS aligns best with your organization’s needs and long-term goals. 

Q&A with experts 

Each AMS Demo Day session includes live Q&A opportunities where you can engage directly with AMS vendors. During this time, you can ask important questions about AMS customization and scalability, implementation timelines and support options, and security and compliance measures. 
 
Participating in these Q&A sessions will give you valuable insights and clarity on which AMS solution is the best fit for your association.

item.title: GoFundMe Nonprofit Scandal: Unauthorized Pages, Hidden Fees, and What Your Organization Needs to Know
item.content:encoded:
Trust is paramount for donors: trust that your organization is keeping expenses low, using secure giving platforms, maintaining strong internal controls and compliance, keeping their personal identifying information private, and funding programs and projects that have the greatest potential for impact.

When donor trust is broken, it can permanently tarnish your organization’s reputation and cause support to dry up.

The recent GoFundMe nonprofit scandal has unnerved organizations, regulators, and donors alike, as it exposed deceptive hidden fees (raising concerns about alleged GoFundMe fraud) and highlighted what some companies feel they can do with publicly available data.

What GoFundMe Did

GoFundMe created 1.4 million unauthorized charity pages for nonprofit organizations, using publicly available IRS information. They were not always accurate. This alleged GoFundMe fraudulent practice allowed pages to accept donations, remain searchable, and operate with hidden fees: GoFundMe's credit card fee, a $0.30 per transaction fee, and a default 14%-16.5% donor tipping model that was routed to GoFundMe. In some cases, these unauthorized charity pages ranked above organizations' actual websites in search results, siphoning intended donors to unaffiliated donation pages.

Why This Qualifies as Fraud and What Regulators Are Saying

GoFundMe, after immediate, intense pushback from the industry and its leaders, walked back these unauthorized actions, apologized, and removed unclaimed pages. This severe misstep has prompted a coalition of 24 Attorneys General and regulators to demand swift action from GoFundMe. GoFundMe allegedly violated state charitable solicitation and consumer protection laws. There is also a pending class action lawsuit against GoFundMe lawsuit that nonprofits are watching closely. are watching closely, as a class action moves forward against the platform.

The GoFundMe Scam Pattern: This Isn't the First Time

There is a history of GoFundMe fraud through individual crowdfunding fundraisers, with bad actors claiming to raise money for individual hardships or as scam disaster relief. While some notable GoFundMe scams have been addressed, it’s possible that some nefarious personal crowdfunding has gone unchecked, given that the platform processes an estimated $50 million a week for individual crowdfunding.

Given its size, GoFundMe fraud hurts overall giving and erodes donor trust, regardless of whether your organization has a relationship with GoFundMe.

What This Means for Your Donors

Due to this industry-wide threat, donors are now confused about where their money goes. GoFundMe’s hidden fees also decrease the actual gift amount nonprofit’s receive compared to what donors were charged. Organizations also cannot access donor information with GoFundMe’s unauthorized charity pages, making stewardship and long-term relationship building impossible. This loss of trust causes real, long-term damage to fundraising.

GoFundMe’s hidden fees have made credit card and software fees now seem unnecessary and suspicious. Yet, security, compliance, transactions, and technology staffing do cost money. Trustworthy technology is worth paying for, but your organization should not face excessive fees.

What to Look for in a Fundraising Platform

When evaluating GoFundMe alternatives, full control of your nonprofit’s fundraising and donor relationship management should be paramount.

Your safe fundraising platform should offer:

[elementor-template id="8984"]

GiveSmart by Momentive Software offers a GoFundMe alternative for nonprofits. We power the full giving experience, from event fundraising to Giving Days and peer-to-peer campaigns. We are committed to transparent, secure technology with donor privacy built in from day one. Connect with our fundraising experts to learn how GiveSmart brings on better for organizations and their donors.

FAQ

Is GoFundMe safe for nonprofits?

In light of recent events, there are serious concerns about GoFundMe's safety for nonprofits. GoFundMe allegedly created 1.4 million unauthorized charity pages using publicly available IRS information — pages that weren't always accurate, could accept donations, and operated with hidden fees. Until these practices are fully resolved and regulatory oversight is in place, nonprofits should proceed with caution and ensure they have full control over any platform representing their organization.

What did GoFundMe do to nonprofits?

GoFundMe allegedly created unauthorized charity pages for nonprofit organizations without their knowledge or consent, using publicly available IRS data. These pages could accept donations, remain searchable, and in some cases ranked above organizations' actual websites in search results — siphoning intended donors to unaffiliated donation pages. After industry pushback, GoFundMe apologized and removed unclaimed pages.

Did GoFundMe get sued?

Yes. A coalition of 24 Attorneys General and regulators demanded swift action from GoFundMe, alleging violations of state charitable solicitation and consumer protection laws. There is also a pending class action lawsuit that nonprofits are watching closely.

What are GoFundMe's fees for nonprofits?

GoFundMe's unauthorized charity pages included several fees: a credit card processing fee, a $0.30 per-transaction fee, and a default tip for donors ranging from 14%–16.5%. The tip, set by the platform, was routed to GoFundMe itself— not the nonprofit. These layered fees were not always clearly disclosed to organizations or donors, raising transparency concerns for nonprofits focused on directing donations to their missions.

Can GoFundMe create a page for my nonprofit without permission?

They did, though they've since reversed course. GoFundMe allegedly created 1.4 million unauthorized charity pages without organizational consent, using publicly available IRS information. Ethical fundraising platforms should require explicit nonprofit consent before launching donation pages or integrating new tools — consent is both a legal safeguard and a sign of respect.

What is the GoFundMe tip, and where does it go?

With GoFundMe's donor tipping model, a tip for GoFundMe was automatically added during checkout. This tip, set by default to 14%–16.5%, is paid to GoFundMe rather than the nonprofit. Donors sometimes assume their total donation goes to the cause, not realizing a portion is retained by the platform. This misunderstanding about where each part of their donation goes can cause confusion or lead donors to abandon their donation mid-process.

What is the best GoFundMe alternative for nonprofits?

When evaluating alternatives, look for platforms that put your organization in control. A safe fundraising platform should offer opt-in, consent-based fundraising pages; controlled staff access; upfront fee transparency; branding and customization options; and donor data reporting and communication tools. It's also worth asking potential platforms about their support hours, what features are included in the base fee, whether donors can voluntarily cover credit card fees, and how frequently the platform is updated. GiveSmart by Momentive is one such alternative, built around transparent pricing and nonprofit-first data practices.

How do I remove an unauthorized GoFundMe page for my nonprofit?

Following significant industry backlash, GoFundMe removed unclaimed unauthorized pages. If you believe an unauthorized page still exists for your organization, you should contact GoFundMe directly to claim or request removal of the page.

item.title: What Is Fund Accounting? The Nonprofit Finance Guide
item.content:encoded:
Managing finances is a critical aspect of nonprofit organizations, and accurate and transparent accounting practices are essential for maintaining trust and accountability. One key accounting approach specifically designed for nonprofits is fund accounting, but what is fund accounting?  

The unique demands of nonprofits require specialized accounting funds based on the income source and the restrictions placed upon them. 

Discover the basics of fund accounting below and learn how to set up your organization for success.  

What is Fund Accounting? 

Fund accounting is a system of record-keeping that segregates resources into categories (funds) based on their source and the restrictions placed upon them. Instead of one giant pot of money, your financial structure is divided into distinct funds that each have their own balance sheet and income statement. 

Core Components of Fund Accounting  

Three major tenets make up the fundamentals of fund accounting:  

Fund Structure: Resources are categorized into three main buckets: Unrestricted (used at the organization’s discretion), Temporarily Restricted (tied to a specific project or timeframe), and Permanently Restricted (endowments where only the interest can be spent).  

Multidimensional Chart of Accounts: To track these funds accurately, your chart of accounts must include a fund segment. This allows you to record every revenue and expense against a specific fund without the need for manual spreadsheets.  

Segregation of Resources: This practice ensures that money meant for a "New Building Fund" is never accidentally used to cover "General Operating" costs. 

Types of Nonprofit Funds  

Your organization’s funds fall into these categories:  

Unrestricted Funds:

These are resources available for the general expenditures of the organization. They are not subject to donor-imposed stipulations, meaning the board of directors and management have full autonomy to use them for any purpose that supports the mission of the entity. 

Restricted Funds:

These represent resources subject to specific external stipulations. They are often divided into two subcategories: 

Temporarily Restricted:

Resources that must be used for a specific purpose (such as a scholarship program) or during a specific time period. 

Permanently Restricted:

Resources that must be maintained in perpetuity, often referred to as the principal. 

Endowment:

A specific type of restricted fund where the original principal investment is intended to remain untouched forever. The organization generally spends only the investment income or a small percentage of the fund’s market value each year to support long-term sustainability. 

Designated Funds:

These are a subset of unrestricted funds that have been "set aside" by the organization's governing board for a specific project or future use. Unlike restricted funds, these are not legally bound by a donor’s request and can be "undesignated" at any time by a board vote. 

Fixed Asset Funds:

This category is used to track the value of the organization’s long-term physical assets, such as land, buildings, and equipment. Segregating these ensures that the "value" tied up in a building is not confused with liquid cash available for monthly bills. 

Fund Accounting vs. General Accounting 

General accounting and fund accounting are two distinct approaches to financial management, each with a specific purpose and application. Here’s a comparison between general accounting and fund accounting:  

Beyond that, fund accounting incorporates several nonprofit-specific requirements, including:  

Focus on compliance: Fund accounting places a strong emphasis on compliance with donor restrictions and tracking the use of restricted funds, whereas general accounting focuses more broadly on financial performance and profitability. 

Reporting requirements: Fund accounting necessitates separate reporting for each fund, providing transparency and accountability for the utilization of funds, while general accounting typically provides consolidated financial statements for the entire organization. 

Strategic Benefits of Fund Accounting 

By segregating financial resources, your organization gains several strategic advantages: 

  • Informed Decision-Making: By analyzing the performance of individual funds, leadership can identify which programs are sustainable and where resources need to be optimized. 
  • Audit Readiness: Maintaining separate audit trails for restricted grants reduces the risk of human error and ensures that financial reviews are seamless and transparent. 
  • Stronger Donor Relations: When you can provide a donor with a report showing exactly how their $5,000 grant was utilized, you foster the trust necessary for continued support. 
  • Budgetary Precision: Fund accounting allows you to allocate resources strategically, ensuring that funds are available for specific projects exactly when they are needed. 

How Fund Accounting Supports Your Board 

For board members and executive leadership, fund accounting informs governance and strategic oversight, providing the transparency required for guiding your mission. 

Most importantly, fund accounting shows a dedication to your donor base. It shows your organization is spending funds for their required purpose.  

For strategic planning, fund accounting eliminates financial confusion. Instead of just final fiscal figures, board members see the totals each fund has, and can determine how to allocate your organization’s resources.  

Additionally, fund accounting facilitates better budgeting. Your board can create designated funds that collect funds for a specific purpose. For example, if your organization has a new capital building goal, the board could create a fund that supports that goal specifically.  

Choosing Fund Accounting Software 

While many nonprofits start with for-profit tools, manual workarounds are often time-consuming and error-prone.  

Fund accounting software is designed specifically for nonprofits and eliminates workarounds and manual data entry, ensuring donor intent is always met. Moving beyond basic transaction entry, these specialized tools provide complete visibility into your organization’s financial health. 

Your organization can benefit from: 

  • Automated Segregation: Transactions route automatically to the correct fund based on predefined rules, eliminating the manual data shuffle and ensuring donor intent is met. 
  • Real-Time Fund Reporting: Instead of waiting for a month-end close, you can generate fund-specific balance sheets, income statements, and FASB-/GASB-compliant reports with a single click. 
  • Encumbrance Accounting: Track commitments before they become actual expenses. This allows you to reserve funds for future obligations, preventing budgetary overspending, 
  • Native Integration: By connecting your accounting system directly to payroll and donor management tools, you create a unified ecosystem with a 360-degree view of institutional health. 
  • Scalable Modules: Modern systems allow you to add specific modules for grant management, procurement, or advanced forecasting as your organization grows. 

Fund accounting software makes it easy to manage your organization's mission, while staying compliant with nonprofit-specific reporting requirements.  

Conclusion 

Fund accounting isn't a complexity upgrade, it's the right foundation for any organization managing restricted funds, grants, or multiple revenue streams. MIP Accounting offers a comprehensive, modular suite designed to handle the most complex fund structures with ease. With 40-plus years' of experience in managing nonprofit finances, MIP helps organizations maintain transparency while funding the one thing that matters most: your mission.  

See how MIP Accounting’s fund accounting software can help your organization today. Request a demo. 

Frequently Asked Questions 

What is fund accounting in simple terms? 

Fund accounting is a specialized system of record keeping that organizes an organization’s money into separate, self-balancing funds based on their source and intended use. Instead of managing one single bank balance, it treats different revenue streams like distinct accounts, each with its own financial statements. This structure ensures that every dollar is tracked according to specific restrictions or project requirements. 

Who uses fund accounting? 

This method is primarily utilized by nonprofit organizations, government agencies, and educational institutions that receive revenue with specific strings attached. Unlike businesses that focus on a single bottom line of profit, these entities must demonstrate that they are using resources for their intended charitable or public purposes. It is also common for foundations and other mission-driven groups that manage diverse grants and endowments. 

What is the difference between fund accounting and regular accounting?  

Regular accounting for for-profit businesses combines all financial activities into a single general ledger to measure overall profitability and performance. In contrast, fund accounting segregates transactions into different funds to ensure compliance with donor-imposed restrictions. While general accounting focuses on a consolidated view for shareholders, fund accounting prioritizes transparency and accountability for specific resources. 

What are the types of funds in nonprofit accounting?  

Nonprofit funds generally fall into categories like unrestricted, restricted, and designated. Unrestricted funds can be used at the organization’s discretion for any operational need, while restricted funds are tied to specific projects or timeframes by external donors. Additionally, organizations may use endowment funds for long-term sustainability or fixed asset funds to track the value of physical property like buildings and equipment. 

Do small nonprofits need fund accounting software?  

Even small nonprofits can benefit from specialized software because manual workarounds in standard tools are often time-consuming and prone to human error. Purpose-built software automates the segregation of resources and provides real-time reporting, which is essential for audit readiness and donor trust. Using a dedicated system from the start allows an organization to scale its financial management as its mission and funding sources grow. 

How does fund accounting support grant compliance?  

Fund accounting creates an automated and permanent audit trail that proves exactly how grant money was spent in accordance with donor requirements. By segregating grant revenue into its own fund, organizations can generate precise reports showing that funds were used for specific program costs rather than general overhead. This level of transparency reduces the risk of non-compliance and strengthens the relationship between the nonprofit and its grantors.

item.title: How Wealth Screening Software Helps Nonprofits Raise More in Less Time
item.content:encoded:
For nonprofits juggling multiple campaigns, events, and donor relationships, knowing where to focus your energy with a small team can make all the difference. That's where wealth screening software comes in.

If your organization relies on gut instinct or spreadsheets to determine how much to ask a donor for, you're likely leaving revenue on the table. Donor wealth screening software changes that and gives you the data to approach every ask with confidence, personalization, and precision.

In this guide, we'll cover: 

  • What wealth screening software is and why nonprofits need it
  • The data provided and how to use it
  • Key features to look for and how to choose the right platform
  • How to use wealth screening data to improve your fundraising strategy
  • The power of integrated wealth screening, donor CRM, and fundraising software

What Is Wealth Screening Software?

Wealth screening software analyzes publicly available and proprietary data to assess a donor's financial capacity and likelihood of philanthropy. 

By combining financial indicators with philanthropic history and demographic data, wealth-screening software provides a clear, comprehensive picture of each donor, helping teams focus donation efforts where they're most likely to pay off.

Importantly, wealth screening software for nonprofits answers two critical questions:

  • Can this donor give more than they currently do?
  • How likely are they to say yes?

Why Nonprofits Need Wealth Screening Software in 2026

Fundraising has grown more competitive, data-driven, and donor-centric than ever before. Donors now expect personalized communication, timely acknowledgment, and meaningful engagement from the organizations they support. 

Meeting those expectations requires more than good intentions and a few hastily cobbled-together spreadsheets — it requires the right tools.

Here's why donor wealth screening software has become essential for purpose-driven organizations:

You Can't Afford to Research Every Donor Manually

When your team has three people wearing six hats, hours spent manually researching donor financials is time stolen from cultivation, event management, and campaigns. 

Wealth screening software does the research for you — surfacing giving capacity, asset data, and philanthropic history in seconds, so your team spends time on relationships, not spreadsheets.

Disconnected Tools Mean Invisible Donors

If a donor gave at your gala, clicked your email campaign, and attended last year's auction, do you have a single view of that story? For most nonprofits, the answer is no. 

When wealth data, event records, and donation history live in separate systems, high-value donors slip through the cracks. Wealth screening software only delivers its full value when it's connected to the rest of your fundraising ecosystem.

"With GiveSmart, things don't fall through the cracks, unlike when you have multiple spreadsheets and multiple places that people need to look for information... if it's automated, it just happens, and GiveSmart allows us to have most of it in one place.” — Kelly J., Littleton Public Schools Foundation

The Cost of Underasking is a Missed Relationship

Donors expect you to know their capacity. For example, asking a major gift prospect for $250 signals that you haven't done your homework and don’t understand who the donor is. 

Wealth screening software closes that gap, giving you the confidence to make asks that match what donors can (and want) to give.

First-Time Donors Decide Whether to Give Again Within Days

The faster you acknowledge a first gift, the more likely a donor is to make a second one. But speed requires knowing who to prioritize. When wealth screening data flows automatically into your donor CRM, high-capacity new donors surface immediately — so your team can get a personalized thank-you out the door before that window closes.

What Data Does Wealth Screening Software Provide?

The specific data available through wealth screening software can vary by platform, but most platforms provide a combination of the following:

Estimated Giving Capacity

Based on income, assets, and giving history, wealth-screening software calculates a realistic ceiling for what a donor could give, which helps you set ask amounts that are ambitious yet grounded.

Financial Indicators

Real estate ownership, stock holdings, business affiliations, and other assets all signal financial capacity. Wealth screening tools aggregate these signals so you don't have to piece them together manually.

Philanthropic History

Has this donor given to other nonprofits? At what levels? Their giving patterns across the sector are strong predictors of future generosity with your organization. This history helps you understand not just if they'll give, but how they prefer to give.

Demographic and Relationship Data

Age, location, marital status, educational background, and board affiliations all inform how you segment, personalize, and prioritize your outreach. When layered with financial data, demographics round out the complete donor profile.

Key Features to Look for in Wealth Screening Software

Not all wealth screening software is created equal. When evaluating your options, look for these capabilities:

Database depth and accuracy: The more comprehensive the underlying database, the more reliable your wealth indicators will be.

CRM integration: Wealth screening should feed directly into your donor management system rather than sit in a separate silo. Look for native or seamless integrations that automatically update donor profiles.

Batch screening: The ability to screen your entire database — not just individual donors — lets you identify major gift prospects you may have overlooked.

Scoring and segmentation: Beyond raw data, the best donor wealth screening software provides scores or tiers that make it easy to prioritize outreach across your donor base.

Ease of use: Your development team shouldn't need a data science background to interpret results. Look for clean, actionable dashboards.

[elementor-template id="1458"]

Choosing the Right Wealth Screening Software

With several wealth screening tools on the market, it's worth taking a deliberate approach to selecting the right one. The wrong choice can mean paying for data you can't act on — or worse, data that never makes it into the systems your team uses daily. 

Here's a framework for evaluating your options:

Prioritize CRM and Platform Integration Above All Else

The single most important evaluation criterion is whether the wealth screening software integrates natively with your fundraising and donor management platform.

A tool that requires manual exports, spreadsheet uploads, or separate logins creates friction — and friction means the data doesn't get used. 

Ask vendors: Does the integration push data automatically into donor profiles, or does it require a manual process? Is the integration bidirectional? How frequently does it update? 

"I knew we needed something to help us track what's going on and get a clear picture... I can't keep working on spreadsheets that just have a bunch of lists of names, dates, and phone numbers... the fact that I have a tool like this means that I can actually even do it. It wasn't even a possibility beforehand, so we were losing money." — Laurin Moore, Angleton ISD Education Foundation

Evaluate Data Sources and Database Size

A larger, more regularly updated database means more accurate wealth estimates across your full donor base, including mid-level donors who are often overlooked.

Look for providers that pull from a wide range of verified data points. For example, real estate records, SEC filings, business ownership, and philanthropic databases.

Ask vendors: How many records are in your database? How often is it updated? What's the coverage for smaller or regional donors?

Assess Usability for Non-Technical Teams

Your development staff is an expert in fundraising, not in data science. The best wealth screening software for nonprofits translates complex financial data into clear, actionable scores and summaries — without requiring a steep learning curve. 

Look for intuitive dashboards, clear giving capacity tiers, and simple filtering so your team can identify and prioritize high-potential donors in minutes, not hours.

Ask vendors: Can a development coordinator use this without IT support? Is there training included, and what does ongoing support look like?

Understand the Full Cost Picture

Wealth screening software varies widely in pricing — from flat annual fees to per-record or per-screen models. Before signing a contract, understand exactly what you're paying for. For example, some platforms charge extra for batch screening, integrations, or advanced reporting, and others bundle these features into a single transparent price.

For budget-conscious nonprofits, it's also worth calculating the return on investment (ROI). For example, if wealth screening helps you identify even a handful of major gift prospects per year, the ROI typically far outweighs the cost.

Ask vendors: What is included in the pricing? Are integrations, batch screening, and support extra? What are the contract terms?

Look for a Proven Track Record with Nonprofits Like Yours

Wealth screening software built for wealth management firms or financial advisors serves a very different purpose than platforms purpose-built for nonprofits. 

Look for providers with demonstrated experience in the nonprofit sector, strong user reviews on platforms like TrustRadius or G2, and case studies from organizations of a similar size and mission.

[elementor-template id="1526"]

How to Use Wealth Screening Data Effectively

The real value of wealth screening software lies in how your team puts that data to work. Here are four ways to translate insights into action:

Tailor Your Ask Amounts

Use estimated giving capacity to set personalized ask levels in your donation forms, direct mail, and in-person solicitations. An ask aligned with what a donor can realistically give is more likely to be met — and more likely to be respected.

Prioritize Major Gift Cultivation

Not every donor in your database needs the same level of attention. Wealth screening software helps you identify the top 10–20% of your donor base that warrants personal outreach, dedicated stewardship, and major gift conversations.

Segment for Campaigns and Events

Whether you're planning your annual gala, a peer-to-peer campaign, or a year-end giving push, wealth screening data lets you create smarter segments to send the right message, to the right person, at the right time.

Accelerate Donor Acknowledgment

When wealth data flows automatically into your donor CRM, teams can quickly identify high-capacity first-time donors and thank them before the window closes. Faster acknowledgment leads to higher retention — and retention is the most cost-effective path to increased revenue.

Wealth Screening, Donor CRM & Fundraising Integration

When wealth screening software, event tools, donation platforms, and donor records all live in separate systems, the result is manual work, delayed insights, and missed opportunities.

The solution is a connected ecosystem where data flows automatically between tools.

With GiveSmart's integrated platform, donor and transaction data syncs automatically every night, eliminating the manual imports and exports that eat up your team's time and introduce errors. 

When you layer in a wealth screening integration like DonorSearch, that data populates directly into donor profiles, giving your team a complete, real-time picture of each supporter without lifting a finger.

Here's what that connected workflow looks like in practice:

  • A donor attends your gala and makes their first gift through the GiveSmart Events platform
  • Their transaction data automatically syncs into GiveSmart Donor CRM overnight
  • DonorSearch wealth data is accessible within that donor's profile, providing giving capacity, asset indicators, and philanthropic history in one place
  • Your development director identifies a high-capacity, first-time donor and flags them for immediate follow-up
  • A personalized thank-you letter goes out within 48 hours
  • That donor is far more likely to give again (GiveSmart customers see an average 20x return on investment in their first year)

GiveSmart + DonorSearch: Raise More for Your Organization

Most wealth screening tools just give you fragmented data. The GiveSmart + DonorSearch integration gives you data you can actually act on because it lives exactly where your team works, connected to every gift, event, and touchpoint in your donor's history.

Here's what that looks like in practice:

Your Donor's Full Financial Picture, Inside Their Profile 

Net worth estimates, real estate holdings, stock assets, business affiliations, and philanthropic giving history — all populated directly into GiveSmart Donor CRM, without manual data entry—no toggling between platforms. No copy-paste.

Records That Update Themselves 

Every gift made through a GiveSmart event or campaign syncs automatically into the donor CRM overnight. That means DonorSearch wealth data and real-time giving history are always in the same place, always current.

Faster Acknowledgment for Your Highest-Potential Donors 

DonorSearch prospect scoring, combined with GiveSmart's thank-you letter wizard, makes it simple to identify first-time, high-capacity donors and send a personalized acknowledgment out the door before the window closes.

Household-Level Intelligence 

GiveSmart's dynamic Households and Groups feature, which maps the full network of connections around a donor, gets significantly more powerful when wealth data is layered in. You're not just seeing individual capacity. You're seeing family-level giving potential.

Your Best Donors Are Already in Your Database

Most nonprofits are sitting on a goldmine of untapped giving potential — they just don't have the tools to see it. GiveSmart's connected fundraising platform, paired with DonorSearch wealth screening, changes that. Stop leaving major gifts on the table.

[elementor-template id="1464"]

FAQ

What Is Wealth Screening Software?

It analyzes donor financial, demographic, and philanthropic data to estimate giving capacity and likelihood. This helps nonprofits focus their fundraising efforts on the donors most likely to give.

Why Do Nonprofits Need Wealth Screening Software?

It saves time by automating research and surfacing high-potential donors. It also increases revenue by enabling personalized, data-driven asks.

Can Wealth Screening Integrate With My CRM?

Yes, top platforms sync automatically with donor management and fundraising systems. This ensures data stays up-to-date without manual entry. Book a demo to learn more.

What Features Should I Look For in a Platform?

Look for database depth, batch screening, scoring, segmentation, and easy-to-use dashboards. Seamless CRM integration is also critical for efficient workflows.

How Does Integration Boost Fundraising Efficiency?

Connected tools eliminate manual imports and data silos, keeping donor information centralized and current. This saves time and helps teams act on insights faster.

item.title: Onboarding Volunteers the Right Way: Strengthening Safety, Compliance, and the Volunteer Experience
item.content:encoded:
Volunteer onboarding is one of the most important moments in a volunteer’s journey. It’s when organizations welcome people into their mission and set expectations for how volunteers will represent and support the work. 

I recently had the opportunity to explore this topic during a session at the Double the Donation Corporate Volunteering Summit, where I was joined by Jennifer Gleason, Senior Manager of Risk Management at YMCA of the USA. Together, we discussed how organizations can design onboarding processes that support safe, well-managed volunteer programs while also creating meaningful experiences for volunteers. 

The YMCA operates one of the largest volunteer networks in the country, serving communities through local YMCAs nationwide. Because many YMCA programs involve youth, families, and other vulnerable populations, the organization has developed thoughtful frameworks for volunteer onboarding, safety, and risk management. These practices offer valuable insights for nonprofits of all sizes that want to strengthen their volunteer programs. 

During our session, we explored practical ways organizations can approach volunteer onboarding, from defining volunteer roles to documenting compliance and training with nonprofit software. Below are several key ideas nonprofits can apply to their own programs. 

Understanding the Purpose of Volunteer Onboarding 

Volunteer onboarding serves several important purposes for organizations and the communities they support. 

First, onboarding helps organizations gather the basic information needed to manage volunteers effectively. Applications, contact information, and scheduling details allow teams to coordinate activities and communicate clearly with volunteers. 

Second, onboarding provides the opportunity to screen, train, and prepare volunteers so they can serve effectively and responsibly. Volunteers represent the organization and interact with the people it serves, which means organizations must ensure they are properly vetted and understand expectations. 

Third, onboarding supports volunteer engagement. When volunteers understand their role, receive the right training, and feel well matched to the work they are doing, they are far more likely to return and deepen their involvement. Many organizations find that volunteers who have positive experiences become long-term supporters, advocates, or donors. 

Strong onboarding processes create the foundation for volunteer relationships that are safe, productive, and rewarding for everyone involved. 

A Framework for Evaluating Volunteer Risk 

One of the most helpful ways to structure volunteer onboarding is by defining risk profiles for different volunteer roles. This approach helps organizations determine the appropriate level of screening, training, and oversight for each role. 

Four factors often guide these decisions: 

1. Legal and regulatory requirements 

Some volunteer requirements are defined by laws, regulations, or governing bodies. These may come from federal or state regulations, insurance providers, or national organizations that set standards for affiliated groups. 

For example, organizations involved in youth sports may need to follow federal regulations related to background checks and safety training. Many nonprofits also work with insurers or accreditation bodies that outline volunteer safety requirements. 

Understanding these requirements is an important first step in building an onboarding process. 

2. Who volunteers interact with 

The population volunteers serve can significantly influence risk considerations. 

Organizations often take additional precautions when volunteers interact with: 

  • Children and youth 
  • Older adults 
  • Patients in healthcare settings 
  • Survivors of abuse or trauma 
  • Individuals with physical or cognitive vulnerabilities 

These situations may require more extensive screening, training, and supervision to ensure the safety and well-being of everyone involved. 

3. What volunteers are asked to do 

The tasks volunteers perform can introduce additional safety considerations or training needs. 

Examples include: 

  • Food pantry volunteers who need food safety guidance 
  • Volunteer drivers who must provide valid driver’s licenses 
  • Youth sports coaches who require concussion awareness training 
  • Program volunteers who work directly with participants in educational or mentorship settings 

Clearly defining responsibilities allows organizations to determine the preparation and oversight required for each role. 

4. Level of supervision and autonomy 

The level of independence volunteers have also plays a role in determining onboarding requirements. 

Volunteers working alongside staff in highly supervised environments typically carry different risk considerations than those who: 

  • Lead activities independently 
  • Supervise participants 
  • Make program decisions in the field 

Organizations often evaluate the full context of each role, including situations that may occur occasionally rather than regularly. Even limited moments of unsupervised interaction can influence onboarding and training requirements. 

How the YMCA Structures Volunteer Risk Profiles 

During our session, Jennifer Gleason shared how YMCA of the USA works with local YMCAs across the country to support consistent volunteer safety standards. 

Many YMCA programs involve youth and families, so the organization categorizes volunteer roles according to levels of access. This framework helps determine what screening, training, and documentation volunteers should complete before beginning their service. 

The YMCA generally groups volunteer roles into three categories. 

Low-access volunteers 

Low-access volunteers interact with program participants in limited or highly supervised settings. 

Jennifer shared the example of volunteers helping with a community 5K event. While children and families may attend, volunteers are typically working in public spaces, and participants remain accompanied by parents or guardians. 

Common onboarding elements for these roles may include: 

  • Volunteer applications 
  • Code of conduct agreements 
  • Liability waivers 
  • Basic identity information for recordkeeping 

Even in lower-access roles, organizations benefit from collecting volunteer information and documenting participation. 

Medium-access volunteers 

Medium-access volunteers may not interact directly with program participants but still hold positions of trust within the organization. 

Board members are a common example. While they may not work directly with youth programs, they often have access to financial information, governance decisions, and organizational strategy. 

For these roles, many organizations conduct additional vetting steps, including: 

  • Background checks 
  • Interviews 
  • Reference checks 

These measures help organizations demonstrate responsible oversight and protect both the organization and its reputation. 

High-access volunteers 

High-access volunteers interact regularly with vulnerable populations and often build ongoing relationships with program participants. 

Examples include: 

  • Youth sports coaches 
  • Mentors 
  • Volunteers working regularly in youth programs or senior services 

In many cases, these volunteers perform responsibilities similar to staff members. Organizations often apply comparable onboarding standards, such as: 

  • Background checks 
  • Reference checks 
  • Interviews 
  • Required safety or abuse-prevention training 

A layered screening process helps organizations create safe environments and ensures volunteers are prepared for their responsibilities. 

Why Multiple Screening Methods Matter 

Background checks are an important component of volunteer screening, but they are most effective when combined with other steps in the onboarding process. 

Interviews and reference checks can provide additional insights into a volunteer’s experience, interests, and suitability for a role. Training programs help volunteers understand expectations, safety procedures, and best practices for interacting with participants. 

Together, these elements create a more complete onboarding process that prepares volunteers to serve responsibly and effectively. 

Clear communication about screening and training requirements also signals that the organization prioritizes safety and accountability. 

Considerations for Corporate Volunteer Groups 

Corporate volunteer programs bring unique opportunities and considerations for nonprofit organizations. 

Many nonprofits partner with companies that organize volunteer days or service projects for employees. These programs can introduce large groups of volunteers to an organization in a single event. 

Organizations often evaluate how these volunteer activities fit into existing risk profiles. Some nonprofits verify screening or training processes used by corporate partners. Others design volunteer opportunities that fall into lower-risk categories, such as facility improvements or outdoor projects. 

In either case, collecting basic information about participants remains valuable. Maintaining records such as volunteer names, contact information, service dates, and project details supports both operational coordination and relationship building. 

These records also allow organizations to follow up with volunteers, share impact stories, and invite individuals to participate in future opportunities. 

The Importance of Documentation 

Many organizations invest significant effort in developing strong volunteer policies and onboarding practices. Maintaining documentation is equally important. 

Records help organizations demonstrate that required steps, such as background checks, waivers, or training, have been completed. Good recordkeeping also provides operational insights, helping organizations understand who participated in volunteer activities and how programs are evolving. 

Maintaining clear documentation supports both compliance and long-term program development. 

Key Takeaways for Nonprofit Leaders 

Organizations looking to strengthen their volunteer onboarding processes can start with several practical steps: 

  • Define volunteer roles clearly 
    Document responsibilities, populations served, and supervision levels for each role. 
  • Develop risk-based onboarding standards 
    Apply appropriate screening, training, and documentation requirements based on the role. 
  • Use multiple screening and preparation methods 
    Combine background checks, interviews, reference checks, and training where appropriate. 
  • Maintain clear documentation 
    Keep records of applications, waivers, training completion, and volunteer participation. 
  • Create positive volunteer experiences 
    Match volunteers with roles that align with their interests and skills to support long-term engagement. 

Volunteer programs succeed when people feel welcomed, prepared, and supported in the work they are doing. 

Thoughtful onboarding processes help organizations protect their communities, empower volunteers, and create experiences that encourage people to stay involved. When volunteers feel confident in their role and connected to the mission, they are more likely to return, deepen their involvement, and continue supporting the organization’s work for years to come. 

Save time and make deeper connections with the people who fuel your mission. Discover how volunteer management software can help with onboarding, enforcing background checks, managing document verification, and more.
item.excerpt:encoded: Guide to volunteer onboarding, from risk profiling, background checks to training & documentation. Make sure your program is safe, compliant & engaging.
