item.title: Talent Retention Strategies That Actually Work in 2026
item.content:encoded:
In a landscape defined by rising employee expectations, volunteer burnout, and intensifying competition for skilled professionals, talent retention has become one of the most pressing operational challenges across industries.  

Talent retention strategies are the deliberate actions organizations take to keep their best people engaged, productive, and committed over the long term. This guide covers proven, practical strategies for retaining employees, volunteers, and association members — the three audiences most organizations can't afford to lose. 

What Is a Talent Retention Strategy? 

A talent retention strategy is a structured set of policies, programs, and cultural practices designed to reduce turnover and keep high-value contributors engaged with your organization. 

Retention strategy used to live squarely in HR departments, focused primarily on salary and benefits. That definition is too narrow for today's reality.  

Employers, nonprofits, and membership associations are all rethinking how they retain the people they depend on — whether that's a full-time employee, a dedicated volunteer, or a dues-paying member. In each case, the core challenge is the same: people have more options, higher expectations, and less patience for organizations that don't invest in their experience. 

A strong retention strategy addresses not just why people leave, but why they stay, and deliberately builds more of those conditions to encourage long-term retention. 

Why Talent Retention Is Getting Harder 

Reducing turnover has never been more difficult, and the reasons go deeper than compensation. Four forces are converging to make workforce retention a persistent challenge: 

1. Burnout is Endemic 

Years of doing more with less has depleted workers across sectors. According to Momentive's 2026 Workforce Report, burnout consistently ranks among the top reasons employees and volunteers disengage before formally leaving. 

[elementor-template id="1458"]

2. Career Development Gaps are Widening

People don't just want a job; they want a trajectory. When organizations fail to show a clear path forward, ambitious contributors begin looking for one elsewhere. 

3. Disconnected Technology Creates Daily Friction

Outdated or fragmented systems frustrate staff and volunteers alike. When the tools people use daily make their work harder rather than easier, it erodes satisfaction in ways that are easy to overlook until someone hands in their notice. 

4. Expectations Have Risen Significantly

Flexibility, psychological safety, purpose-driven work, and recognition are no longer perks; they are baseline expectations for a large portion of the modern workforce. 

Understanding these dynamics is step one. Building retention best practices around them is where organizations make real progress. 

Talent Retention Strategies for Employees 

Employee retention strategies work best when they address the full employee experience, not just the moment someone considers leaving. Here are five evidence-backed approaches your organization can adopt today to boost talent retention: 

1. Make Career Paths Visible 

One of the most powerful staff retention tips is deceptively simple: show people where they can go. Employees who can see a clear internal growth path are significantly less likely to look externally. This means documented career ladders, regular development conversations with managers, and internal job postings that prioritize internal candidates. A well-integrated career center platform can help employees explore opportunities within your organization before they start searching outside of it. 

2. Invest in Manager Quality 

The research is unambiguous: people leave managers, not companies. Manager effectiveness is one of the strongest predictors of retention at every level. Investing in manager training, feedback mechanisms, and accountability for team health pays dividends across the entire workforce. Organizations that treat manager quality as a retention lever, not just a performance metric, consistently outperform peers in how to retain employees over the long term. 

3. Offer Genuine Flexibility 

Flexible work arrangements remain one of the most cited reasons employees stay or leave. This doesn't mean every role needs to be remote, but it does mean organizations should audit their flexibility policies honestly. Where flexibility isn't possible, transparency about why, and what else the organization offers, goes a long way toward maintaining trust. 

4. Build Recognition into the Rhythm of Work 

Recognition doesn't require a formal program, though those help. What matters most is frequency and specificity. Employees who feel seen and appreciated for their specific contributions are far more engaged than those who receive generic praise. Train managers to recognize early and often and build peer-recognition mechanisms that don't rely entirely on the hierarchy. 

5. Reduce Technology Friction 

Few things erode employee satisfaction faster than systems that make work harder. Auditing your technology stack for unnecessary friction — redundant logins, manual processes that should be automated, disconnected tools — is one of the highest-ROI retention investments available. A purpose-built career centers platform can connect employees to development resources, job opportunities, and mentorship in one streamlined experience, reducing the friction between ambition and action. 

Talent Retention Strategies for Volunteers 

Volunteer retention deserves the same strategic attention as staff retention — but it often doesn't get it. Volunteers who feel underutilized, under-communicated with, or overwhelmed by administrative burden quietly stop showing up. These strategies help reverse that pattern: 

1. Assign Roles That Match Motivation 

The single most common reason volunteers disengage is role mismatch — being asked to do tasks that don't align with why they got involved in the first place. Intake processes that capture volunteer skills, interests, and availability, and then actually use that data, dramatically improve retention. When volunteers feel their time is being used meaningfully, they give more of it. 

2. Communicate Consistently and With Purpose 

Sporadic communication is a retention killer for volunteers. Regular touchpoints — whether that's a monthly update, a recognition shoutout, or a quick check-in before a major event — signal that the organization values the relationship, not just the labor. The organizations with the best volunteer retention rates treat communication as ongoing relationship maintenance, not just logistics. 

3. Create Belonging, Not Just Belonging-Adjacent Activities 

Retention for volunteers is deeply tied to community. Volunteers who feel like they're part of something — who have relationships with other volunteers, feel recognized by staff, and understand the impact of their work — stay far longer. Recognition events, team-based volunteer assignments, and visible impact reporting all contribute to this sense of belonging. 

4. Reduce the Administrative Burden on Coordinators 

Volunteer coordinators are often the unsung retention lever, or liability. When they're buried in spreadsheets and manual scheduling, they have no bandwidth to invest in volunteer relationships. Purpose-built volunteer management software automates scheduling, communication, and reporting so coordinators can focus on what actually retains volunteers: connection, recognition, and meaningful role management. 

Retention Strategies for Association Members 

Member retention strategies for associations operate on a different cadence than employee or volunteer retention, but the underlying drivers are similar. Members renew when they feel the value of membership is concrete; the community is real, and the organization actively invests in their success. 

1. Make Value Impossible to Miss 

The number one reason members don't renew is a perceived lack of value, not a lack of actual value. Associations often deliver tremendous ROI but fail to communicate it clearly. Renewal campaigns, year-in-review reports, and member dashboards that surface personalized engagement data (events attended, resources accessed, connections made) dramatically improve retention by making the value visible. 

2. Build Genuine Community 

Networking is why many professionals join associations. When that promise is fulfilled — through active peer groups, regional chapters, mentorship programs, or online communities — retention soars. When it isn't, members feel they're paying for a newsletter. Investing in community infrastructure is one of the most durable member retention strategies available. 

3. Create Engagement Touchpoints Throughout the Year 

Retention risk spikes when members go 90+ days without meaningful engagement. A deliberate engagement calendar, webinars, roundtables, exclusive content releases, check-in emails tied to career milestones, keeps the association present and valuable between the annual conference and the renewal invoice. 

4. Connect Members to Career Development Resources 

Career development is a primary driver of association membership, particularly for early- and mid-career professionals. Associations that offer robust career resources, job boards, resume tools, skills assessments, and mentorship matching, see meaningfully higher retention rates.  
 
An integrated association management software platform combined with a dedicated career centers solution gives associations the infrastructure to deliver these resources at scale, while capturing the engagement data needed to personalize the member experience. 

What the Best Retention Programs Have in Common 

Whether you're retaining employees, volunteers, or members, the programs that work share three fundamental characteristics: 

1. Belongingness 

People stay where they feel seen, connected, and part of something larger than a transaction. This is true across every audience type. Retention programs that invest in community, through recognition, peer connection, and visible impact, consistently outperform those focused purely on transactional incentives.  

2. Visibility into Growth

People need to see a path forward. Employees want career trajectories. Volunteers want to know their contributions are leading somewhere meaningful. Members want to see how the association is helping them advance professionally. When organizations make growth visible, they give people a reason to stay.  

3. Technology that Enables Connection rather than Creating Friction

The best retention tools are the ones people don't have to fight with. Platforms that make it easy to find opportunities, communicate with coordinators, access resources, or connect with peers amplify every other retention investment.  

These aren't abstract principles; they're actionable diagnostic criteria. If your retention rates are struggling, audit your programs against all three. The gap is usually hiding in one of them.  
 
For a deeper look at the data behind these trends, the 2026 Workforce Report provides comprehensive benchmarks across industries, organization types, and audience segments to help you understand where your retention strategy stands — and where the highest-leverage opportunities are. 

Ready to Build a Retention Strategy That Sticks? 

Talent retention is not a problem you solve just once. It's an ongoing investment in the conditions that make people want to stay, and the tools that make it possible to deliver those conditions at scale. 

The organizations that win on retention in the coming years won't necessarily be the ones with the biggest budgets. They'll be the ones that listen to what their people need, build systems that make those needs easier to meet, and use data to continuously improve. 

[elementor-template id="1526"]

FAQ 

What are the most effective talent retention strategies?

The most effective talent retention strategies combine career development visibility, strong manager relationships, meaningful recognition, flexible work arrangements, and technology that reduces daily friction. The right mix depends on your organization type and workforce demographics, but all high-performing retention programs invest in belonging and growth. 

How do you retain employees who are thinking about leaving? 

Start with a stay interview; a structured conversation designed to understand what would make an employee more likely to stay. Address the top issues directly and quickly. For employees earlier in their disengagement, career pathing conversations and workload relief often have the highest impact. Acting early is far more effective than counter-offering once someone has accepted a new position. 

What is the difference between employee retention and talent retention? 

Employee retention typically refers to keeping paid staff within an organization. Talent retention is a broader concept that includes any high-value contributor — employees, yes, but also volunteers, members, contractors, and others. The strategies overlap significantly, but talent retention acknowledges that organizations depend on a wider network of contributors than just full-time staff. 

Why is volunteer retention so difficult? 

Volunteer retention is difficult because organizations often treat volunteers as a means to an end rather than as contributors with needs of their own. Volunteers who feel their time is wasted on administrative tasks, who receive inconsistent communication, or whose skills are mismatched with their roles tend to quietly disengage. Investing in volunteer coordination infrastructure and role-matching dramatically improves outcomes. 

What metrics should organizations track for retention? 

Key retention metrics include overall turnover rate, tenure distribution, first-year turnover (a strong indicator of onboarding effectiveness), regrettable vs. non-regrettable attrition, and engagement scores. For associations and nonprofits, member renewal rates, volunteer return rates, and engagement frequency are the most predictive leading indicators. 

How does technology impact talent retention? 

Technology has a dual impact on retention. When it works well, it removes friction, connects people to opportunities and each other, and makes growth visible. When it doesn't, it creates daily frustration that erodes satisfaction in ways leaders often underestimate. Purpose-built platforms for career development, volunteer management, and association management consistently outperform generic tools because they're designed around the specific workflows that matter most to retention.

item.title: Creative Charity Dinner Fundraising Ideas & Themes for 2026
item.content:encoded:
A successful charity dinner fundraiser combines a compelling mission, a well-targeted audience, and clear opportunities to give, with dinner and entertainment components that encourage fellowship. 

This is one of the most effective nonprofit fundraising approaches because it blends relationship-building and fundraising into a single experience. Creating an emotional connection through storytelling, while making donations easy and instant, is crucial.

Donors are more likely to give when they feel connected, inspired, and part of a shared purpose. To that end, we’ve filled this guide with our favorite fundraising dinner themes and charity dinner ideas.

What Makes a Great Charity Dinner Fundraiser? 

A successful charity dinner fundraiser hinges on several key elements: 

  • The appropriate venue for your cause and supporters 
  • Seamless registration and ticketing for your attendees and volunteers
  • A well-paced program that keeps guests engaged
  • Donation opportunities (think: paddle raises or fund-a-need appeals) held when energy and emotion are highest
  • Entertainment/speakers that reinforce your mission and make the experience memorable

Top Charity Dinner Fundraising Ideas 

So, what are some of our top charity dinner fundraising ideas? We’ve compiled a list of the most successful:

  • Live Auction – Auction high-value items or experiences with an energetic auctioneer to drive competitive bidding and large donations.
  • Silent Auction – Guests bid on items via paper or mobile platforms throughout the evening, maximizing participation without interrupting the program. 
  • Fund-a-Need Appeal – Directly ask for donations tied to specific impact, giving donors something tangible to see where their money goes. For example, “Donating just $500 will help 10 families celebrate the holiday.” 
  • Paddle Raise – A fast-paced donation moment where guests raise paddles to commit funds publicly, building momentum and social validation.
  • Wine Pull – Guests purchase a mystery bottle of wine at a fixed price, often receiving a bottle worth more than they paid. Bonus: The bottles are often donated, so it is all profit!
  • Dessert Auction – Tables bid on premium desserts, adding a fun, social, competitive element late in the evening.
  • Table Captains – Assign each table a host who can explain your mission or campaign and encourage guests during donation moments.
  • Live Entertainment + Donation Moments – Integrate performances with short, emotional appeals to capture attention and prompt immediate giving.
  • VIP Experiences – Offer premium ticket tiers with perks like exclusive seating, meet-and-greets, or private receptions to increase revenue per guest. Bonus: If you can wrangle a celebrity chef or a local restaurant’s chef, you can offer a chef’s table or special menu selections!
  • Raffle Drawings – Sell tickets for a chance to win various prizes. Raffles are simple to run and effective for broad participation. Often, nonprofits will offer raffle tickets at a low price point, hoping to maximize participation.
  • Peer-to-Peer Pre-Event Campaigns – Empower supporters to fundraise before the event, building momentum and increasing donations before guests even arrive. 

Many of these nonprofit fundraising dinner ideas have virtual capabilities that can be enabled with the right software. From auctions and raffles to VIP meet-and-greets and paddle raises, purpose-built software can turn any of these ideas into hybrid events or even fully virtual dinner fundraising events.

[elementor-template id="1526"]

Fundraising Dinner Themes That Drive Attendance and Donations 

Nonprofits and supporters alike love a theme. Fun themes can get attendees excited and in the mindset to donate before they even set foot into the event. Some more successful themes have been:

  • Black-Tie Gala – A classic, upscale experience that signals importance and encourages higher ticket prices and major gifts. Donors expect premium experiences and are primed to give generously.
  • Casino Night – Interactive games such as poker and blackjack keep guests engaged and spending throughout the evening. The playful competition creates multiple opportunities to give beyond the ticket price.
  • Italian Dinner or International Potluck – Appealing to a specific culture or encouraging the sharing of different cultures is often a successful theme. It is much easier to justify the cost of the ticket when you know the food will be amazing, or you will be able to try something you've never had before.
  • Decade-Themed Party – Nostalgia and costumes make any event more fun and memorable, increasing attendance and participation. Who doesn’t love an 80’s party? Guests are more relaxed and willing to engage in fundraising activities.
  • Mission-Based Theme (Cause-Driven Experience) – For example, if you’re raising funds for an art program, you could host a student art showcase so that donors can see the impact of their donations firsthand. These themed events really reinforce supporters' emotional connection to your cause.

Nonprofit Dinner Fundraiser Tips to Maximize Revenue 

To maximize revenue at your charity dinner fundraiser: 

  • Offer tiered pricing, from general admission to VIP-sponsored tables, to create various giving levels and maximize revenue. 
  • Structure your event’s program to build momentum by placing high-impact donation moments—like a fund-a-need or paddle raise—after an emotional story or testimonial. 
  • Use mobile bidding and text-to-donate tools to make giving fast, seamless, and accessible from any seat. 
  • Train your volunteers to guide guests throughout the event, answer questions about your organization or fundraising campaign, and promote participation during key donation moments. 
  • After the event, send personalized thank-yous, impact updates, and further giving opportunities.

Charity dinner events are among the most effective ways to fundraise. They allow your nonprofit the opportunity to promote your mission, interact with supporters, and drive donations. With the right tools and strategy, these dinners can provide both immediate revenue and long-term donors. 

GiveSmart’s fundraising software can help your team streamline ticketing and registration, host live and silent auctions (in-person or virtual), collect and track donations, and manage events. 

[elementor-template id="1458"]

FAQs

What is a charity dinner fundraiser?

The basic description of a charity dinner fundraiser is: A ticketed event where attendees enjoy a meal while supporting a cause. The goal of these events is to raise money while cultivating strong relationships with supporters. Charity dinner fundraisers often involve a cocktail or gathering hour, speeches from those close to the cause, some form of music or entertainment, and donation opportunities, such as auctions or raffles.

How do you plan a successful fundraising dinner?

To plan a successful fundraising dinner, you’ll need to be organized and locked in to your mission and supporters. Also:
- Set clear goals
- Create a budget
- Determine your target audience
- Choose a venue and date 
- Secure sponsors
- Plan interesting and engaging programming
- Promote the event early through multiple channels.

What are the best fundraising dinner themes for nonprofits?

Popular themes include: 
- Galas
- Cultural or international cuisine
- Decade-themed (Disco, 80s, etc.)
- Casino night
- Cause-related themes tied to your mission (fundraising for an art program could be an art show with pieces by the students)

How much should nonprofits charge for charity dinner tickets?

Tickets should be priced based on event costs and fundraising/campaign goals, as well as the giving potential of your audience. You can also offer tiered pricing with options such as general admission, VIP, or a sponsored table.

What fundraising activities work best during a dinner event?

To bolster excitement and encourage participation, consider including interactive elements, such as:
- Raffles 
- Live & silent auctions
- Paddle raises
- Direct appeals 
- Employer/sponsor matching times

How can nonprofits increase donations at a charity dinner?

Communicate the impact of donations through storytelling or testimonials. Make giving easy by offering options, such as mobile bidding or text-to-donate. Live donation thermometers showcase how close you are to your fundraising goal and can encourage donations. Also, real-time shoutouts to donors can inspire others to add their donation during live appeals. Learn more strategies to maximize donations.

item.title: Nonprofit Career Guide: Paths, Skills, and Jobs That Make a Difference
item.content:encoded:
People who work for nonprofits do so because their work directly improves the lives of others and benefits the greater good. While competitive salaries are still important, people pursuing nonprofit work often feel a personal bond with the organization’s mission or cause.  

Pursuing a career in the nonprofit space is about more than just a paycheck; it’s a chance to create lasting change for a social or environmental cause.  

But getting started in the nonprofit space and finding suitable candidates for nonprofit jobs can be difficult. This nonprofit career guide explores the career paths available, the essential expertise needed to succeed, and how you can build a rewarding career that makes a lasting difference. 

Looking to support nonprofit talent at your association? Explore how Career Center Software can help you connect members with the right opportunities. 

What Is a Nonprofit Career? 

A nonprofit career involves working for an organization that reinvests all surplus revenue back into its mission rather than distributing it to shareholders.  

Nonprofits have several career paths available, spanning every department a for-profit business does, including marketing, finance, program management, development, and more. 

Common Nonprofit Types  

There are several different types of nonprofits that span virtually every career sector or interest. Some common nonprofit types include:  

  • Charities: Direct service providers that focus on immediate needs like food security, housing, or healthcare 
  • Associations: Membership-based groups, such as professional societies or trade unions, that support the interests of a specific industry or community 
  • NGOs (Non-Governmental Organizations): Entities that address global issues like human rights, disaster relief, and sustainable development. 
  • Foundations: Organizations that primarily provide funding and grants to other nonprofits and community initiatives. 

Is a Nonprofit Career Right for You? 

A nonprofit career is rewarding in many ways, from the scope of the work to the pride team members feel working for such an organization. Determining if a nonprofit career is right for you means examining your motivations and professional expectations.  

While your organization’s mission is an important driver, nonprofit careers come with unique operational challenges.  

Challenges of a Nonprofit  

It is essential to approach a nonprofit career with a clear understanding of the common hurdles: 

  • Resource constraints: Many organizations operate with lean budgets, requiring staff to be creative and resourceful with limited tools. 
  • Multiple roles: Job descriptions are fluid and require pivoting with little notice. You may find yourself managing a project one hour and helping with event logistics or administrative tasks the next. 
  • Compensation and burnout: While many nonprofits offer competitive pay, salaries still trail behind the corporate sector. Additionally, the emotional weight of mission-driven work can lead to burnout if healthy boundaries are not maintained. 

If you’re looking to evaluate whether nonprofit work is the right fit, consider these questions:  

  • Values alignment: Does the organization’s mission align with your personal values? 
  • Tolerance for uncertainty: If an economy or a societal change creates uncertainty, do you enjoy adapting to uncertainty and finding ways to succeed despite it?  
  • Collaborative spirit: Do you enjoy working in largely team-based work environments?  
  • Adaptability: Are you comfortable stepping outside your primary area of expertise to solve immediate problems? 

Nonprofit Career Paths 

Every career specialty has a role in a nonprofit. Some of the most common roles fall into distinct nonprofit career paths, like program and service career management, or events and volunteer coordinators, among others. These are some of the most common nonprofit career paths:   

Program and Services Careers 

A career path in programs and services is the heartbeat of any nonprofit because they’re focused on delivering the organization’s mission directly to its beneficiaries.  

  • Sample roles: Program Coordinator, Case Manager, Program Director, Director of Impact. 
  • Responsibilities: You will manage daily operations, track service metrics, and ensure programs meet your mission.  
  • Progression: Career growth often moves from coordinating specific initiatives to overseeing entire departments and setting the strategic direction for regional or national programs. 

Fundraising and Development Careers 

The development department ensures the financial sustainability of an organization by building relationships with donors to secure donations. 

  • Roles: Development Assistant, Development Director, Major Gifts Officer, Grant Writer, Director of Annual Giving. 
  • Responsibilities: These positions involve identifying funding opportunities, writing persuasive proposals, and managing donor stewardship. 
  • Progression: Most begin as a Development Assistant or Grant Writer, move into specialized roles like Major Gifts Officer, and eventually transition into leadership as a Director of Development or Chief Advancement Officer responsible for the total revenue goal. 
  • Technology Integration: Modern nonprofits use tools like GiveSmart to simplify and streamline fundraising, mobile bidding, peer-to-peer campaigns, and analyzing donor data to drive better engagement and fundraising outcomes. 

Events and Volunteer Management Careers 

These careers focus on mobilizing people and executing high-impact gatherings to raise awareness and support. 

  • Roles: Event Coordinator, Volunteer Manager, Director of Special Events. 
  • Responsibilities: You will recruit and train volunteers, manage logistics for large-scale conferences, and create memorable experiences for supporters. 
  • Progression: Early roles like Volunteer Coordinator or Event Assistant lead into Special Events Manager positions. Senior professionals move into Director of Community engagement or Director of Events roles, overseeing large-scale conferences and national volunteer networks. 
  • Technology integration: Again, technology helps simplify the work in this space. Platforms like VolunteerMatters assist with volunteer coordination, while platforms like Expo Logic, Core-Apps, and TripBuilder Media streamline event registration, conference work, and attendee mobile experiences. 

Association Careers 

These roles are centered on supporting the professional or personal growth of a specific community or professional group. 

  • Roles: Member Services Specialist, Chapter Relations Manager, Director of Career Services. 
  • Responsibilities: Tasks include managing membership renewals, providing educational resources, and fostering a sense of community across local chapters. 
  • Progression: Starting in Member Services, you can progress to a Chapter Relations Manager and eventually to a Director of Membership or VP of Association Strategy, where you focus on high-level retention and member value propositions. 
  • Technology integration: Association management software has made it simple to manage membership and grow non-dues revenues. Platforms like YourMembership, Nimble AMS, Aptify, and NetForum to manage member data, while YM Careers gives Associations a tool to provide job boards and professional development tools. Plus, platforms like CrowdWisdom, Freestone, and BlueSky give associations another tool to help member retention while powering online learning and professional certification programs. 

Marketing and Communications Careers 

This path focuses on telling the organization's story to inspire action and build a strong brand. 

  • Roles: Digital Marketing Manager, Content Strategist, Communications Director, Press Secretary. 
  • Responsibilities: You will manage social media, draft press releases, and create multi-channel campaigns that elevate the mission and attract new supporters. 
  • Progression: You may start as a Communications Coordinator or Social Media Specialist, grow into a Marketing Manager role, and ultimately reach the executive level as a Communications Director or Chief Marketing Officer responsible for global brand and advocacy. 

Finance and Operations Careers 

These professionals provide the structural foundation that allows a nonprofit to function efficiently and transparently. 

  • Roles: Finance Manager, Staff Accountant, HR Director, Chief Operating Officer. 
  • Responsibilities: Key duties include budget management, human resources, and ensuring the organization remains compliant with tax and labor regulations. 
  • Progression: This path usually moves from Staff Accountant or HR Generalist to Finance Manager or Operations Director. Top-tier roles include Chief Financial Officer (CFO) or Chief Operating Officer (COO), focused on organizational sustainability and internal health. 
  • Technology integration: Platforms, like MIP Accounting, give finance professionals purpose-built tools to handle the intricacies of nonprofit financial reporting. 

Technology and Data Careers 

As the sector becomes more data-driven, these roles have become essential for optimizing performance and understanding impact. 

  • Roles: CRM Administrator, Data Analyst, LMS Administrator, Product Owner. 
  • Responsibilities: You will manage the technical ecosystem, analyze donor and member behavior, and ensure that digital platforms meet organizational needs. 
  • Progression: Often beginning as a CRM Administrator or LMS Coordinator, you can move into Data Analysis or Digital Strategy Manager roles. Career growth leads to positions like Director of Technology or Chief Information Officer (CIO), overseeing the entire digital ecosystem and data strategy. 
  • Technology integration: Platforms, like MomentiveIQ, empower nonprofits with advanced data insights to streamline operations and increase efficiency.
[elementor-template id="1526"]

Nonprofit Job Types and Responsibilities 

Here’s a list of common nonprofit job types and responsibilities, along with the average salary range, when available: 

Programs and Services 

These professionals focus on the design, execution, and measurement of the organization's mission-critical work. 

  • Program Coordinator: Handles day-to-day administrative tasks and logistics for specific initiatives. Range: $50,000–$76,000 
  • Program Manager: Oversees the strategic implementation and budget of multiple projects or a primary program.  
  • Chief Programs Officer (CPO): High-level executive responsible for the entire program portfolio and long-term impact strategy. Range: $120,000–$199,000 

Fundraising and Development 

This team is responsible for securing the financial resources required to sustain and grow the organization. 

  • Development Associate: Supports donor outreach, maintains the fundraising database, and assists with gift processing. Range: $50,000–$69,000 
  • Grant Writer: Researches and drafts persuasive proposals to secure funding from foundations and government entities. Range: $60,000–$79,000 
  • Major Gifts Officer: Focuses on cultivating and stewarding relationships with high-net-worth individual donors. Range: $77,000–$130,000 

Events and Volunteer Management 

These roles manage the people and the gatherings that foster community engagement and awareness. 

  • Volunteer Coordinator: Recruits, trains, and manages the schedules of volunteers across various programs. Range: $41,000–$71,000 
  • Director of Special Events: Leads the planning and execution of large-scale fundraisers, conferences, and galas. Range: $70,000–$109,000 

Membership and Association 

Focused on providing value and community for member-based nonprofit organizations. 

  • Member Services Specialist: Acts as the primary point of contact for member inquiries and renewal processes. Range: $60,000–$80,000 
  • Association Membership Manager: Manages retention strategies, member benefits, and chapter relations.  

Marketing and Communications 

These professionals manage the messaging and promotion of the nonprofit to drive support.  

  • Marketing Associate: Executes social media posts, email newsletters, and basic website updates. Range: $50,000–$69,000 
  • Communications Manager: Develops the narrative strategy and manages press relations and brand consistency. Range: $60,000–$99,000 

Finance and Operations 

The backbone of the organization, ensuring financial transparency and efficient internal processes. 

  • Staff Accountant: Manages daily financial entries, accounts payable, and payroll. Range: $50,000–$90,000 
  • Director of Finance: Oversees the financial health, audit preparation, and long-term fiscal planning. Range: $100,000–$169,000 
  • Chief Financial Officer (CFO): Executive leader responsible for global financial strategy and sustainability.  

Technology and Data 

Optimizing the digital tools and data analysis that allow the nonprofit to run effectively. 

  • CRM/Data Analyst: Manages the donor and member database to provide actionable insights for development and marketing. Range: $62,000–$97,000 
  • LMS Administrator: Oversees the learning management system for organizations focused on education and certifications. Range: $55,000–$85,000 

Skills, Education, and Certifications for Nonprofit Work 

People who succeed in working for nonprofits often mix interpersonal abilities with a level of tech proficiency. Nonprofits continue to embrace technology to streamline the administrative side of their careers.  

Core Skills for Nonprofit Professionals 

  • Communication and relationship-building: The ability to articulate your organization’s mission and build authentic connections with donors, volunteers, and stakeholders is the foundation of the sector, and one of the most important skills a person working for a nonprofit should have. Your organization’s mission is its lifeblood and the reason why stakeholders support your work. Being able to tell your nonprofit’s story is the most important thing a person working for a nonprofit can do.  
  • Resilience and collaboration: Working in mission-driven environments often requires navigating emotional weight and working across teams to get work done. 
  • Project management: Turning a vision into a reality requires the discipline to manage timelines, budgets, and diverse teams effectively. 

Technical and Digital Skills 

While interpersonal skills are crucial, having a fair degree of technology proficiency is also important. Many organizations rely on purpose-built platforms to ease the strain of work on their team members and fight off burnout. Nonprofits frequently use:  

  • CRM and fundraising platforms: Proficiency in technology platforms, like GiveSmart or donor management tools, is essential for tracking engagement and revenue. 
  • LMS and analytics: Understanding how to manage learning platforms and interpret data, through platforms like MomentiveIQ, allows organizations to measure their impact and quantify how the organization functions.  
  • Marketing tools: Marketing platforms reduce the administrative work needed to promote and market a nonprofit. Tools like email automation, social media scheduling, and content management are vital to build your organization's voice. 

Helpful Degrees and Certifications 

  • Advanced degrees: A Master of Public Administration (MPA) or a specialized degree in Nonprofit Management provides a strong theoretical and practical foundation. 
  • Professional Certifications: Earning a CFRE (Certified Fund Raising Executive) or CAE (Certified Association Executive) signals a high level of expertise and commitment to the field. 
  • Specialized training: Certifications in volunteer management or nonprofit finance (such as those related to MIP) can set you apart in competitive hiring cycles. 

Building Your Skill Stack 

Even if you’re still in school or in a different career space, nonprofits are always seeking out interested parties to support their organization. Some common ways to gain nonprofit experience include:  

  • Volunteering and internships: Hands-on experience at a local nonprofit is the fastest way to understand the operational realities of the sector. 
  • Micro-credentials and online courses: Pursue specific certificates in grant writing, digital marketing, or data analysis to fill gaps in your resume. 

How to Start a Nonprofit Career (Step-by-Step) 

If you’re looking to get into the nonprofit space, these step-by-step instructions can help you identify how to get into the industry: 

  • Clarify your interests: Determine which causes (e.g., healthcare, education, environment) and which functions (e.g., marketing, finance, programs) align with your passions and strengths.  
  • Audit your skills: Map your existing professional experience to nonprofit needs. Often, corporate skills in project management or sales translate directly to program lead or development roles. 
  • Gain relevant experience: If your resume lacks nonprofit history, consider board service or joining a committee to build your sector-specific credibility. 
  • Optimize your presence: Tailor your resume and LinkedIn profile to highlight mission-driven accomplishments and use the language of the nonprofit sector. 
  • Network intentionally: Attend association events and sector-specific conferences to meet leaders and peers in your chosen field. 
  • Search sector job boards: Use association-specific job boards to find roles that might not be listed on general sites. 
  • Prepare for the interview: Be ready to discuss your personal why alongside your professional how in the role. It’s important to show you know the organization and its mission.  

Growing Your Nonprofit Career 

Once you’re established in the space, career progression remains similar to the for-profit world. Here’s two examples of how a career in the nonprofit world can progress:  

  • The Specialist Path: Move from a Grant Writer to a Senior Development Officer, eventually becoming a Director of Foundation Relations. 
  • The Leadership Path: Progress from a Program Coordinator to a Regional Director, ultimately reaching a Chief Operating Officer or Executive Director position. 

Using Learning and Certifications to Advance 

Continuing education is a primary driver of upward mobility. In today’s modern world, websites like LinkedIn, HubSpot Academy, and other similar platforms provide multiple opportunities to pursue CE credits online. Plus, professional societies often have deep learning management opportunities and even industry conferences to help boost your credentials.  

Moving Into Leadership and Executive Roles 

Executive leadership in a nonprofit requires a shift from tactical execution to long-term strategic vision, board governance, and high-level financial stewardship, when appropriate. As you work through your career in the nonprofit space, you’ll continue to add responsibilities.  

Leverage your organization’s existing leadership and learn from them. As they retire or move to other organizations, your nonprofit’s board will shift responsibilities to you until eventually they appoint you to an interim or permanent role.  

Guidance for Hiring Managers & Associations to Attract Nonprofit Talent

This section explores how hiring managers can leverage modern career centers, refine their messaging, and build internal structures that not only attract high-quality candidates but also foster long-term retention and organizational health. Some top-line principles include:  

  • Write better job descriptions: Focus on the impact the role will have and clearly define the day in the life to attract candidates who align with your culture.  
  • Showcase mission and culture: Modern candidates prioritize values. Highlight your organization's successes and your commitment to staff well-being. 
  • Use specialized career centers: Use specialty job-boards, like those powered by YM Careers, to reach a highly qualified pool of professionals who are specifically looking for association and nonprofit opportunities. 

Building Career Paths Inside Your Organization 

Prevent turnover by creating career ladders that provide a clear path to promotion. Mentoring programs can further bridge the gap between junior staff and leadership. 

Supporting Continuous Learning and Development 

Utilize a Learning Management System (LMS) to provide your staff with on-demand training. This not only upskills your workforce but also demonstrates an investment in their professional longevity. 

Your Next Step in a Nonprofit Career 

Building a career in the nonprofit sector is a journey of aligning your professional expertise with a cause that matters. Whether you are just starting out or looking to move into executive leadership, the transition from a traditional role to a mission-driven one requires a strategic approach to your skills, networking, and technology. 

Key Takeaways 

  • Mission matters: Success in this sector is defined by impact and a personal connection to the organization’s goals. 
  • Skill stacking is essential: Combine core interpersonal skills like relationship-building with technical proficiency in CRMs, fundraising platforms, and data analytics. 
  • Continuous learning drives growth: Utilize certifications like the CAE or CFRE and engage with modern learning management systems to stay ahead of industry trends. 
  • Specialized search works Best: General job boards often miss the nuances of the sector. Focus your search on association and nonprofit-specific platforms for the best results.  

For Job Seekers

Are you ready to find a role where your work makes a tangible difference? Skip the generic search engines and head to a relevant industry association to find a professional job board with roles tailored to the industry you’re looking to break into.  

For Associations and Hiring Managers

Building a pipeline of high-quality talent is the foundation of a sustainable organization. Learn how Momentive Software powers the entire talent journey—from sophisticated job boards and career centers to integrated learning management systems that keep your staff engaged and growing. 

[elementor-template id="1458"]

FAQ 

How much do nonprofit jobs pay compared to the private sector?

Nonprofit salaries are typically lower than private-sector roles, but the gap varies by position and organization size. Many nonprofits offset this with strong benefits, flexibility, and mission-driven work.

Do I need a degree to work for a nonprofit?

Not always. Many roles value experience, skills, and passion for the mission. Some positions (like finance, research, or leadership) may prefer or require a relevant degree.

What is the best certification for nonprofit professionals? (CFRE, CAE)

CFRE is ideal for fundraising and development professionals, while CAE is best for association and nonprofit management leaders. The right choice depends on your career path.

How do I transition from a corporate job to a nonprofit career?

Start by volunteering, highlighting transferable skills, networking in the sector, and tailoring your resume to emphasize mission-driven impact and collaboration.

What nonprofit jobs are in the highest demand right now?

Fundraising and development, grant writing, program management, marketing/communications, data and technology roles, and finance/operations are currently in high demand.

item.title: 10 Essential Tips for Executive Directors to Drive Nonprofit Success
item.content:encoded:
Executive Directors are responsible for A LOT at a nonprofit organization. From managing staff to liaising with the board and vetting vendors to leading the fundraising, marketing, and mission delivery staff, Executive Directors play a significant role in the day-to-day operations and long-term success of their nonprofits.

An Executive Director’s effective leadership is tethered to achieving an organization’s short and long-term goals. These tips will help any Executive Director frame their plans for success and growth.

Tip #1

Clearly Define and Communicate the Mission

Storytelling is integral to nonprofit sustainability. Without being able to tie everything your organization does back to your mission via the right voice, branding, design, and writing, your nonprofit may falter with donor retention and revenue generation.  

With so much wasted time on manual computer work and redundancies, crafting the story and brand doesn’t get the dedication it deserves from Executive Directors. Your mission is critical; give yourself the time to dedicate to telling your organization’s story.  

Tip #2

Develop and Implement a Strategic Vision

Executive Directors are ultimately responsible for the strategic vision for organizational decisions. What does success look like for your organization? The strategic vision includes things like evaluating and implementing changes to programming, staffing, vendors, policies, long-term goals, and benchmarks of success. 

Bringing the strategic vision to your staff and board allows for a focused, driven team working toward mission growth and sustainability.  

Tip #3

Build a Strong and Committed Team

Hiring and retaining talented, qualified individuals who align with the mission is something Executive Directors need to get right. Fostering a positive and collaborative organizational culture ultimately starts and ends with an Executive Director. 

While most nonprofits are increasing staff with qualified hires, staff, and high-level volunteer retention remains mission critical. Investing the time and energy into your staff will pay off in stronger donor, volunteer, and corporate relationships. 

Tip #4

Establish Strong Board Relations

Executive Directors are responsible for board recruitment, development, and management. While board governance responsibilities vary, an Executive Director nevertheless must make board engagement a top priority of their role.  

Board members have often been supporters and sustainers for a longer tenure than the average Executive Director. Board of Directors, when managed right, can be tied to, either through direct giving or by extension, the near majority of your organization’s fundraising, event, and sponsorship revenue.  

Building a collaborative relationship with the board chair, committee leaders, and members at large is vital for any Executive Director’s success.  

Tip #5

Diversify Funding Streams 

Sustainable funding and effective financial management play a vital role in any nonprofit. With the majority of nonprofit leaders looking to add more diversity to their funding streams, an Executive Director is at the helm for developing strategies for grant applications, corporate partnerships, major giving, fundraising campaigns, and legacy giving.  

An Executive Director will ensure long-term sustainability by optimizing these efforts via an investment in donor stewardship and improved financial tracking and management.  

Tip #6

Embrace Technology and Innovation 

Executive Directors are major stakeholders in choosing, leveraging, and providing feedback on technology vendors. You know more than anyone else how inefficient technology wastes your team’s limited time. 

GiveSmart and MIP, both by Momentive Software, allow nonprofits to consolidate vendors and realize efficiencies through financial and donor data. Together, these platforms meet the individual needs of nonprofits from inception, through growth, to maturity, and ultimately foster financial stability and propel missions forward.  

Tip #7

Foster Community and Stakeholder Relationships

Long-term success at any nonprofit hinges on strong donor stewardship, volunteer engagement, and community involvement. Engaging stakeholders in your organization’s mission, campaigns, and fundraising is arguably the most fun part of any Executive Director’s job. Those moments can also be some of the most challenging and time-consuming. 

Finding efficiencies in your day-to-day allows for this cornerstone of any Executive Director’s role to flourish. 

Tip #8

Focus on Impact and Outcomes

No Executive Directors find success in their roles by missing goals and declining revenue. With obvious challenges over the last few years, success has been hard fought. It is a large part of the job to seek and manage effective campaign and budget monitoring and evaluation to help support sustainable growth. It is a larger part of the job to communicate wins and opportunities to your donors, board, and constituents.  

Tip #9

Navigate Challenges with Resilience 

Every nonprofit Executive Director has faced challenges directly related to their role with staff, constituents, performance, and more. While some challenges crop up due to unforeseen circumstances, many others are preventable or can drive a positive change. 

Being able to easily adjust budgets, add to your fundraising calendar, and be on-target with state, county, and federal audit standards helps Executive Directors overcome challenges and find stability.  

Tip #10

Professional Development

Executive Directors are not only responsible for staffing their organization, but they also are key players in staff retention and development. Inarguably, pay at a nonprofit is not a selling point. However, the close exposure to many facets of business–from budgeting to board and staff management–even for more junior staff is a major professional benefit of working at a nonprofit. Working for a mission-focused organization can be life-changing.  

Give your staff ample opportunity for exposure to different parts of your organization, encourage them to carve out time for certification classes, and lean on your board of directors to bring in high-level speakers and new opportunities for learning.  

Executive Directors create and cultivate the right culture for their organization. Doing so makes it most likely that they are surrounded by engaged staff and dedicated donors. These tips will help Executive Directors drive success at their organization and best fuel their missions.

item.title: 3 TikTok Tips for Nonprofits
item.content:encoded:
What is TikTok? 

TikTok is a short-form, video-sharing app that allows users to create and share up to three-minute videos on any topic. 

In recent years, TikTok became one of the most downloaded social media apps. TikTok is only the fifth non-gaming app to reach the three-billion download level. 

In addition, as of January 2022, TikTok had more than one billion monthly active users worldwide. Amongst these statistics, Statista found that TikTok users watch 167 million hours of videos on the app every minute.  

Did you know? In 2021, TikTok ranked in the top 10 list of platforms responsible for driving traffic from social media to GiveSmart fundraising event sites. And 85% of marketers rank short-form videos as the most effective type of social media content. 

These stats combined prove that TikTok has a large number of possible donors ready to support your mission! 

How can you use TikTok for your nonprofit? 

With users spending more than 14 hours per month on the app (Statista), your organization has the potential to reach users multiple times. The question is, how exactly can your nonprofit organization leverage TikTok to gain more donors? 

Let’s run through three tips on how your nonprofit can use TikTok: 

1. Don’t Forget Hashtags 

TikTok is no longer just for dance videos. The platform allows for all types of individual and business creators to take the stage, get creative, share their stories, gain supporters, and make changes in the world. 

Hashtags are still a big deal on social channels, especially TikTok. TikTok’s algorithm depends heavily on users’ hashtag interests. Hashtags are also essential for helping users discover your content via search. 

Along with hashtags, there’s some evidence that suggests trending topics are more likely to end up on a user’s unique “For You” page (also known as the FYP) or the curated home page for videos related to their likes, interests, and interactions on previous videos or topics. So, it can be worthwhile to watch for what is currently trending and hop into the conversation with related content, but make sure you stay authentic to your mission! 

It’s easy to find what is trending. All you need to do to find out which hashtags are trending is tap on the “Discover” tab and then tap ”Trends” at the top of the screen. 

2. You Don’t Always Need to Keep It Short 

Not a lot of users know this (and TikTok will not come out and say it) but like all businesses, TikTok needs to sustainably grow its revenue. To sustain its growth, it needs longer videos to promote to its users  to sell more ad space. Now, we know this sounds like a negative take, but the best place to be is on the good side of the algorithm in the social media realm. 

Should you always create the maximum length allowed? No. But including a few long-form videos that are related to the hashtags being used can help get the most return from your time and effort. 

3. Find Your Audience 

Roughly 50% of TikTok’s global audience is under the age of 34. 

There are specific subgenres of users for everyone.. You need to figure out whom you want to be engaging with your content. 

A great way to find your target audience is to observe your peers in those spaces and see what hashtags they’re using, and the format being used for content. Use those for your inspiration to get those in that space engaged with you! 

Commenting and liking can also help you build relationships with your specific audience. The hopes are that your responses will inspire others to check out what kind of content you’re creating on your page. 

Don’t forget that building relationships with individuals with large followings (aka “Influencers”) can vastly expand your reach. Find influencers with content or missions that align with yours, get to know them, and encourage them to help you promote your mission. 

The best way to gain exposure and build a movement is to be as active as you can with those that are most likely to support you and convert them into ambassadors to help expand your mission. 

Look at GiveSmart customer the Sacramento History Museum on TikTok. Their account has over two million followers (and counting). The content they produce is diverse, engaging, and fits into a niche that applies to their mission. 

Need some inspiration for TikTok content? 

To be effective on any social media channel, your content needs to be timely and relevant to your followers’ current focuses. The more relevant your organization can be to your followers, the more potential for donor growth. 

If you’re looking to get some inspiration or an overall planning guide, check out our Annual Fundraising Calendar. The 2022 GiveSmart Annual Fundraising Calendar delivers the major holidays, recognized awareness moments, days of giving and connection, and fun days (mostly food-based) worth celebrating and can help you plan and tailor your content throughout the year. 

We can’t wait to watch you create and reach your fundraising goals!

item.title: Leveraging Fundraising and Donor Data: Tips from the Experts
item.content:encoded:
Feeling drowned by data? With nearly three out of four organizations being concerned about their long-term sustainability, understanding the makeup and motivations of donors is more important than ever.  

We’ve brought together a panel of partners, ready with ideas to help your team understand the importance of analyzing their past campaigns and events.  

We asked our panel, “How should organizations use their donor and fundraising data to impact giving?” Experts from our on-site event management, donor engagement, and marketing partners shared tips for data collection, auction planning, stewardship, and more.  

With the right donor segmentation plan in place, you’re sure to deepen your donor relationships, stabilizing revenue and increasing growth as you refine your strategy.

Better nurture and engage your donors with GiveSmart’s Go-To Guide for Donor Segmentation 

Grace Green, Senior Partnership Specialist at Double the Donation

One of the most critical pieces of data an organization can access is an individual’s employer information, directly impacting their potential for matching gifts. That’s why it’s recommended to have donors supply the names of their employing companies directly within the giving process itself. 

From there, you have the information to segment your database according to donors’ matching gift eligibility, enabling increasingly targeted outreach and tailored communications. For example, you’d send John Doe, who works for Home Depot, a link to his program guidelines and online submission forms with encouragement to follow the outlined steps to request a match. Meanwhile, Jane Smith, whose employer does not yet match gifts, would receive information about how she can advocate for the company to launch such a program. 

Ryan Scanlan, Co-Founder at GOAT Events

Organizations should be analyzing metrics such as average donation amount, auction item success rates, top contributors, and the distribution of giving levels to help them be strategic when planning silent and live auctions, instant items, and other fundraising components. 

The goal is to create an event experience that resonates with your attendees, encouraging them to contribute in ways that feel most comfortable to them. Remember, every contribution counts, and understanding your audience through data can maximize the impact of their giving. 

Allison Geisinger, Vice President of Development and Events at Powered by Professionals

Nonprofits should use donor data for communication purposes after the event and throughout the year to keep people engaged. Storytelling with impact data in solicitation materials and at your events can help sustain giving all year long. 

Christian Scott, Partner Director at LIFE Event Staffing

One of the biggest hurdles a nonprofit has to overcome is getting past the ideology of “but this is the way we’ve always done it.”  

Taking a hard look at fundraising data can let an organization know where their money is coming from and allow them to see if their choices are working. If you do a golf tournament every year that barely brings in donations, is it worth continuing or is there possibly a better event to engage donors and your community to go in that slot?

As you look to better strategize your donor engagement, turn back to these expert tips from our panel of partners for inspiration.  

At GiveSmart, we’re grateful to power thousands of donations annually. Check out how our donor management software best supports your nonprofit’s donor stewardship and retention.

item.title: 3 Tips to Elevate Member Career Advancement Opportunities
item.content:encoded:
When it comes to attracting and retaining members, career advancement is a big factor. Really big. 

Research shows that job opportunities and career development benefits are an essential offering and are becoming increasingly important to association members. Studies also show that these benefits are especially valued by members who are early in their careers. Emphasizing career development support provides greater value to members. 

Since job opportunities and career advancement benefits are particularly important to those early in their careers, offering career-building opportunities is a great way to attract young members to your association. 

These findings indicate that elevating career advancement opportunities for your members is a great way to provide greater member value and ultimately attract and retain more members. 

Three Ways To Elevate Career Advancement Opportunities For Your Members 

With the right technology and proven approaches, you can create an online career center that supports members' career planning and development. Here are three tips and some powerful resources to help you elevate career advancement opportunities for your members: 

1. Take Your Online Career Center To The Next Level 

If you think about your online career center as just a place for job postings, then it’s time to think again. Your online career center also offers a great opportunity to engage more with members and provide them with the career-building resources they want. So, be sure to provide more than just a job board by also offering a variety of career advancement resources. 

[elementor-template id="1467"]

Here are some examples of career content and resources that you can add to your YM Careers-powered online career center to give members the support and know-how they’re seeking: 

  • Résumé review. Offer free résumé critique through your career center. 
  • Job seeker articles, tips, and recommendations. Offer articles and other content with career advice, including résumé writing tips, interview techniques, job search ideas, and tips on social networking. 
  • Career coaching. Connect members with career coaches to help with things like career direction, job search strategies, and interview tips. 
  • Industry career insights. Provide career data to help job seekers learn more about their current career stage and possible future roles, including information on qualifications, education, certification requirements, expected salary, and more. 
  • Career path mapping. Give your members an online tool to map out their career paths based on the jobs they want, providing them with a well-defined path to get there, including the skills, learning, and job titles they need to reach their goals. 
[elementor-template id="1458"]

Of course, offering job opportunities and career advancement resources is just one part of the equation. It’s also important to ensure members are aware of the career resources you offer. 

The challenge is breaking through the ads and emails that compete for your members’ attention. But there are effective ways to do it. For example: 

  • Send personalized emails to educate members about available resources based on their career stage. 
  • Host informational videos on your website and social media channels to build brand recognition as an online career destination for professionals in your industry. 
  • Post a jobs widget on your organization’s home page to share relevant jobs with people visiting your website and drive awareness for your association’s online career center. 
  • Share social media posts about the career resources available on your association’s job board. Newly posted jobs can also be automatically posted on your social media feeds to deliver relevant content for followers. 

2. Invest In Your Association’s Training And Certification Programs 

Take time to review your association’s training and certification programs, and be sure to include career advancement opportunities in your meetings and conferences. At the same time, remember that it’s important to engage members in a variety of year-round learning opportunities that address their budgets, schedules, educational and certification needs, and learning preferences. 

3. Keep Networking Alive And Thriving 

Your members are seeking networking opportunities to advance their careers. So, be sure to offer networking events and other opportunities, and promote them in your member recruitment and onboarding activities. You’ll attract new members to your organization and get members more involved for years to come. Here are some ideas to help members connect throughout the year: 

  • Plan networking events. These career events can be happy hours, lunches, discussion groups, career fairs, or any other type of event focused on connecting members with thought leaders in your industry, with hiring managers, and with your organization’s staff. 
  • Get social. Integrate your career center with social media so job seekers who view a job on your job board can see any LinkedIn connections who work at the company. This is a useful technology integration that can help them open doors to new opportunities. 
  • Offer an online member community. Become part of your members’ daily social networking habits by providing an online community where members can find industry information, network with your organization and other members, and share ideas. Look for technology that supports a modern online experience and includes tools to help you keep members engaged. 
  • Don’t forget your annual conference. Your annual conference is a great opportunity for members to connect with one another and with others in your industry. Set aside specific times in your conference schedule for networking activities. 

More Ways To Offer Career Advancement Opportunities For Your Members 

By elevating career advancement opportunities, your association can offer members the tools, resources, and insights they need to research, plan, and develop their careers. Discover more tips and insights for offering career advancement opportunities to your members: Read the guide, How to Give Your Members the Career Resources They Want.
